metadata.from: Bob (Robert) Hanifen — signed 'Bobby,' writing to his father

metadata.mood_tone: Casual and conversational; somewhat lighthearted but includes a vivid and unsettling account of a nearby accidental shooting; generally optimistic about coming home soon
metadata.legibility_notes: Handwriting is cursive and somewhat difficult in places; several words required guessing. 'General Supply' is inferred from 'Genered Supply' or similar. The word 'rating' may refer to a discharge rating or points. 'safe place' has a crossed-out word before it. Page 2 has some faded/bleed-through areas.
metadata.historical_context: Written in June 1946, post-WWII. Bob appears to be still serving overseas during the occupation period, waiting for his discharge rating to come through. He mentions locating graves of unknown soldiers, reflecting post-war graves registration work. The accidental shooting incident involving a .45 pistol reflects the dangers of peacetime military life with weapons still present.

pages.transcription:
7 June 1946

Dear Dad
    I received your package today and everything was in good shape. I am not doing much now. The office I used to work in closed down, so now I am working with my buddie at Genered Supply. Just killing time for two week till my rating come through. If it does, I am going to send a package in a couple of weeks with presents for different people. You open it alone. Inside will be a 45, you put it up for me in a [safe?] safe place. Wed. afternoon a kid two bags from me about 36 feet was shot by another kid messing around with a 45. The slug entered his right

side and came out the left side hitting his arm at the elbow and then glance off two walls. The bullet just missed his heart by 1/4 of inch. He is okay now, but I seen him a couple seconds after it happen and it wasn't very pretty. The Blood was on his legs all over the floor. My buddie Lou is transferring to [illegible] ome. He is going to be an interpreter, locating graves of unknown soldiers. Pretty good deal. Hope everything is okay at the now. It is getting mighty hot around here, believe me. Well that is about all the news.
        So long for now
            Lots of love Your Son
                Bobby

P.S. Is Jimmy out of the Army yet?

full_transcription:
7 June 1946

Dear Dad
    I received your package today and everything was in good shape. I am not doing much now. The office I used to work in closed down, so now I am working with my buddie at Genered Supply. Just killing time for two week till my rating come through. If it does, I am going to send a package in a couple of weeks with presents for different people. You open it alone. Inside will be a 45, you put it up for me in a [safe?] safe place. Wed. afternoon a kid two bags from me about 36 feet was shot by another kid messing around with a 45. The slug entered his right side and came out the left side hitting his arm at the elbow and then glance off two walls. The bullet just missed his heart by 1/4 of inch. He is okay now, but I seen him a couple seconds after it happen and it wasn't very pretty. The Blood was on his legs all over the floor. My buddie Lou is transferring to [illegible] ome. He is going to be an interpreter, locating graves of unknown soldiers. Pretty good deal. Hope everything is okay at the now. It is getting mighty hot around here, believe me. Well that is about all the news.
        So long for now
            Lots of love Your Son

P.S. Is Jimmy out of the Army yet?
summary: Written on June 7, 1946, Bob writes to his father from an overseas posting during the post-WWII occupation period. He thanks his dad for the package, mentions his office closed and he is now working with a buddy at General Supply, killing time waiting for his discharge rating. He plans to send a package home including a .45 pistol for safekeeping. He recounts a frightening accidental shooting incident in which a nearby soldier was shot through the chest with a .45, narrowly missing his heart. He closes by noting his buddy Lou is transferring to work as an interpreter locating graves of unknown soldiers, and adds a postscript asking if his brother Jimmy is out of the Army yet.
